dialogue.utterance: Unit 2 to 3145 Eukaria Court for unconscious male.
Hi, I'm Jordan. We're here from the rescue squad. I understand you found a gentleman down.
Yes, he was unconscious on the floor when I came home.
Does he respond to any verbal commands or painful stimuli at the moment?
No, he isn't moving. He's been like this for about 20 minutes.
His eyes aren't open. I'd say his pupils are pinpoint.
Airway seems patent. Can you hold gently to monitor any airway compromise while I assess further?
Shallow and slightly snoring respirations. I'm going to bring on an oxygen mask and provide breath support as needed.
His respirations at this time appear to be 23 per minute unassisted.
His pulse is palpable and strong at 80 beats per minute.
Blood pressure is high - 182 over 95.
Skin is cool, dry, and normal in color.
Radial pulses are strong and present bilaterally.
I'm reviewing the skin and found two abrasions on the top right side of the head.
No signs of swelling or deviation in the trachea. Pupils are still pinpoint.
We are going to administer 2 milligrams of naloxone intravenously to reverse the suspected opioid intoxication.
Blood sugar cannot be assessed at this time.
Did anyone see him before he passed out? Any medical concerns we should know about?
No, I was the first one to find him. I don't think there's any paraphernalia.
Do you know his medical history or medications?
No, we have no idea. No bracelets, no medication bottles.
We are going to insert an airway from the nasal pharyngeal route now.
Does he seem to be experiencing any pain or discomfort?
He's passed out and doesn't seem to be in pain. Just unconscious.
Can you hold a pen and squeeze it? If you can feel it, squeeze once.
No motor response. No noticeable gross movement upon noxious stimulus.
Pupils remain pinpoint again. Head exam still unremarkable in size or shape.
Exiting to medical - overdose/poisoning - opioid (Protocol 7 - 1) per field impressions and initial naloxone response.
The EKG shows a normal sinus rhythm at initial evaluation.
His pulse has increased to 100 per minute. Respirations have dropped to 8. Blood pressure is 167/83.
SpO2 reads 94.
Pulse is still elevated at 100 and EKG now shows sinus tachycardia.
Breathing still shallow and assisted. Airway patent with oxygen support.
We may be considering another round of naloxone, but at this time vitals have remained relatively stable. Let's monitor him.
We are going to transport him to [COMPANY_NAME]. That's the closest facility or is there another preference?
We will be stabilizing him on the stretcher and using five straps to ensure he's secure during transport.
Any other changes in condition or signs we should look for?
No, he was just out cold when I found him. No crying, nothing.
Understood. Let's proceed with securing for transport and reevaluating en route.
Vital signs just updated: 86 pulse. Respirations 18. Blood pressure 131/76.
SpO2 at 92.
EKG still shows normal sinus rhythm now.
His capillary refill is less than 2 seconds and skin turgor remains normal.
He does not appear to be near fainting currently, consistent with signs of unresponsiveness found on arrival.
Patient handed over to ED staff in room 18. Field treatments, medications, and vital changes have been charted.
ED now has full chart data. No need for further interhospital transfers.
Airway remains patent with assisting via BVM.
We have placed him on a bag valve mask to assist breathing until spontaneous efforts return.
Started a four-lead EKG monitoring. It shows sinus rhythm but we'll monitor closely for any changes.
SpO2 was 99% initially after administration of high-flow oxygen.
Blood glucose was not obtained due to no acceptable vein or equipment available on scene.
Patient was found in an unconscious state.
Oxygen saturation after initial intervention is at 99% on a supplemental oxygen mask.
He does not appear to be experiencing any pain or discomfort at this time.
He is being carried to stretcher by three providers and secured for transport.
